mnemonics.title: FILE ALLOCATION METHODS
mnemonics.content: CONTIGUOUS: Fast but Fragments (external). LINKED: Flexible but Fragments pointers. INDEXED: Quick Random Access but wastes Index space.

mnemonics.title: FREE SPACE MANAGEMENT TECHNIQUES
mnemonics.content: BLOC: Bit vector, Linked list, Ordered (grouping), Counting - remember as B-L-O-C.

mnemonics.title: INODE POINTER TYPES
mnemonics.content: DID: Direct, Indirect, Double - three levels of pointing for files to grow.

mnemonics.title: FILE ACCESS METHODS
mnemonics.content: SAD: Sequential, Access (Direct), Indexed - the three ways to read files.

acronyms.usage: A table that tracks which clusters belong to each file, like a chain linking file blocks together
A data structure in UNIX that stores file metadata and pointers to data blocks
The basic unit of storage allocation in a file system, typically 4KB in size
A layer providing a common interface for different file system implementations
File-level storage accessed over a network, implementing file sharing protocols
File system that spans multiple servers, enabling file sharing across networks

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a book where all pages of each chapter are printed on consecutive pages. You can jump to any page quickly, but if you need to add pages, you must move the entire chapter.
Think of a treasure hunt where each clue points to the next location. Each block contains a pointer to the next block - flexible but you must follow the chain.
Like a book index at the back - the index block is like the table of contents showing which page (block) contains each topic (data).
Imagine a parking lot with each spot having a light - green (free) or red (occupied). The bit vector is like a row of lights showing all spots at once.
Think of a library catalog with books organized by call numbers - you calculate where the book should be and go directly there instead of searching every shelf.

analogies.concept: File System to Office Filing Cabinet
analogies.analogy: Just as a filing cabinet organizes papers into folders and drawers, a file system organizes data into files and directories on disk.
A buffer cache is like keeping frequently used documents on your desk rather than walking to the filing cabinet every time you need them - faster access to frequently used items.
The superblock is like the first page of an encyclopedia set - it tells you how the whole system is organized before you start reading.
A mount point is like a doorway into a new room - it connects the main house (root filesystem) to an extension (new filesystem) seamlessly.

analogies.concept: File Locking to Meeting Room Booking
analogies.analogy: File locking is like a meeting room booking system - it prevents two teams from using the same room (file) at the same time, avoiding conflicts.
